nativeRange.habitat: Shallow to mid-depth tropical coral reefs; found on hard substrate across a wide depth range in areas of moderate water movement and varied light intensity

decorPreferences: open spacing from all neighboring corals (minimum 20–30 cm clearance)
stable mounting surface — heavy LPS colonies must be secured against tipping

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiosis. Weekly target feeding with small meaty foods — mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, microplankton, or zooplankton-based coral foods — is generally advised to support growth and coloration. Polyps tend to extend at night or in low-flow conditions to capture food; spot-feeding with a turkey baster or pipette is a commonly recommended technique.

goodWith: reef fish (clownfish, gobies, blennies, peaceful wrasses)
other Torch Coral (Euphyllia glabrescens) colonies with adequate spacing — intraspecific tolerance has been reported, though not guaranteed
non-aggressive LPS corals placed well out of sweeper tentacle range (30+ cm)
avoidWith: Hammer Coral (Fimbriaphyllia ancora / Euphyllia parancora) — Torch Coral is documented to sting and damage Hammer corals; this is one of the most common pitfalls in mixed Euphyllia displays
Frogspawn Coral (Fimbriaphyllia divisa / Fimbriaphyllia paradivisa) — similar risk; Torch sweeper tentacles are longer and more potent than Hammer or Frogspawn
Branching Hammer Coral (Fimbriaphyllia paraancora) — same incompatibility as wall hammer
SPS corals placed within sweeper range
any coral or invertebrate within 20–30 cm at night
large angelfish that may nip at extended polyps
butterflyfish known to nip at LPS polyps

summary: The Torch Coral is a striking LPS coral bearing long, tubular tentacles with distinctive bulbous tips that sway in gentle current. The most aggressive of the popular Euphyllia trio — keep well separated from Hammer and Frogspawn. Advanced care; demands stable chemistry and generous spacing.
careNotes:
TAXONOMY NOTE — 2017 RECLASSIFICATION (CRITICAL FOR REEFERS):
A 2017 molecular phylogenetics study (Luzon et al.) reorganized the former Euphyllia genus, elevating the subgenus Fimbriaphyllia to full genus status and establishing the family Euphylliidae. Torch Coral (Euphyllia glabrescens) was among the species that REMAINED in genus Euphyllia. By contrast, Hammer Coral (now Fimbriaphyllia ancora), Branching Hammer (Fimbriaphyllia paraancora), Frogspawn (Fimbriaphyllia divisa), and Branching Frogspawn (Fimbriaphyllia paradivisa) were ALL moved to Fimbriaphyllia. The practical implication is that Torch Coral and Hammer/Frogspawn are no longer in the same genus — and their aggression profiles reflect this: Torch Corals tend to be considerably more aggressive toward other Euphyllia-type corals than Hammer and Frogspawn are toward each other.

TORCH vs. HAMMER/FROGSPAWN INCOMPATIBILITY (A WELL-KNOWN REEFER PITFALL):
The aquarium hobby commonly groups Torch, Hammer, and Frogspawn as 'Euphyllia corals' with similar husbandry, leading many hobbyists to assume they can be housed together with adequate spacing. This assumption is dangerously misleading for Torch corals specifically:
• Hammer + Frogspawn: these two are broadly considered compatible with appropriate spacing (10–15 cm); documented to coexist and even touch without serious stinging in established tanks.
• Torch + Hammer or Frogspawn: Torch Corals have been consistently documented stinging and damaging or killing Hammer and Frogspawn colonies. The Torch's sweeper tentacles are longer (up to 25+ cm at night) and appear to carry a more potent sting. Even 20–30 cm spacing has not reliably prevented contact-related tissue damage.
The Hammer Coral entry (sw-coral-018) also documents this incompatibility. Both entries should be considered cross-reference for this key fact.
Intraspecific (Torch-to-Torch) tolerance has been reported in some accounts, but is not guaranteed and should be monitored carefully; physical contact between colonies should be avoided.

SWEEPER TENTACLES & AGGRESSION:
Torch Corals deploy sweeper tentacles substantially longer than those of Hammer or Frogspawn — commonly cited at 15–25+ cm (6–10+ inches) at night when the colony is actively hunting. Spacing of at least 20 cm from any other coral is the minimum commonly advised; 30 cm is more conservative and accounts for the colony's growth over time. Plan for the colony to expand significantly — a frag-sized 5 cm colony can reach 30–50+ cm over several years under good conditions.

LIGHTING:
A PAR range of 75–150 is most consistently cited across sources, with the task specification, ReefChasers, and BRS Euphyllia guide all consistent in this range. Torch Corals are generally placed in the middle of the aquarium rather than the upper third, which differentiates them from higher-light SPS corals. Acclimating new specimens gradually — starting at the lower end of the PAR range and moving them to target position over 2–4 weeks — is generally advised to prevent bleaching.

FLOW:
Moderate, indirect, non-laminar water movement is preferred. Direct powerhead or wave pump flow aimed at the coral tends to cause polyp retraction, tissue irritation, and reduced feeding response. A gentle swaying motion of the tentacles is the commonly cited target; many reefers position the coral where it receives indirect flow from a return pump or prop pump on a wide sweep.

WATER CHEMISTRY:
Calcium (400–450 ppm), alkalinity (8–10 dKH), and magnesium (1250–1400 ppm, standard reef) are consistent across sources and align with other Euphyllia entries. Alkalinity stability is particularly important — sudden swings of more than 0.5–1 dKH per day can trigger polyp recession and tissue damage. Two-part dosing or a calcium reactor is advisable for established colonies.

BROWN JELLY DISEASE (BJD):
Torch Corals are considered susceptible to Brown Jelly Disease, a bacterial infection characterized by a brown slimy coating that rapidly dissolves tissue. Physical damage from stings by neighboring corals, shipping stress, or direct high-velocity flow is the most common trigger. Prompt action — removing affected tissue, performing a freshwater or saltwater dip, and physically removing the slime layer — is generally recommended to prevent spread to healthy tissue. Antibiotic treatments (Ciprofloxacin, Amoxycillin) and iodine dips have been reported as helpful in early-stage cases.

FRAGGING:
Torch Coral colonies branch naturally and individual heads can be fragged by cutting between branches at the base. This is more approachable than fragging fused-skeleton LPS corals (like wall hammer), as each branch can be snipped with coral cutters. Frags heal readily on plugs or rubble. Gold, orange, and other rare color morphs ('Gold Torch', 'Bling Torch', 'Indo Gold Torch') command high prices and are propagated primarily from aquaculture frags rather than wild collection.

CROSS-SOURCE DISAGREEMENT NOTES:
• Temperature: ReefChasers 77–84°F (25–28.9°C); FishkeepingWorld 78°F as a point value. Averaged to 25–28°C, consistent with general tropical reef ranges and the Hammer Coral entry.
• Care level: ReefChasers 'advanced'; FishkeepingWorld 'moderate'; task specification 'intermediate-advanced'. 'Advanced' adopted, consistent with task specification and the species' disease susceptibility and aggression management requirements.
• Minimum tank size: FishkeepingWorld 50 gal (189 L). Rounded up to 200 L (the nearest nice round liter value), as the colony's sweeper tentacle range demands significant clearance space.
• Sweeper tentacle length: Task specification 15–25+ cm; this is consistent with hobby accounts and the biology of the species. FishkeepingWorld did not quantify; BRS and ReefChasers described them qualitatively as 'long.' Range documented in careNotes as 15–25+ cm.
• Adult colony size: FishkeepingWorld states 4–5 inches (10–13 cm); this appears to describe a single polyp head rather than a mature multi-head colony. Mature colonies in captivity commonly reach 30–60 cm across multiple heads; 15–60 cm used as the colony range.
breedingNotes: Torch Corals can be fragged by cutting between individual branch heads with coral cutters or bone cutters; each head grows on a distinct stalk and can be separated and mounted independently on frag plugs or rubble. Frags as small as a single head heal readily under good water conditions. In the wild, Euphyllia glabrescens reproduces sexually via broadcast spawning (mass-spawning events tied to lunar cycles, similar to other Scleractinian corals), but deliberate captive sexual reproduction is not practiced in the reef aquarium hobby.

primary.name: ReefChasers – Torch Coral Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary source. Covers: temperature 77–84°F; pH 8.0–8.4; alkalinity 8–9 dKH; calcium 400–450 ppm; lighting MODERATE 100–150 PAR; flow moderate indirect; care level ADVANCED; compatibility: do not recommend keeping with Hammers or Frogspawns; Torch-to-Torch tolerance reported; Brown Jelly Disease susceptibility documented. Salinity, magnesium, and tank size not specified.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – A Euphoric Ecoscape of Euphyllias
additional.notes: Cross-checked Euphyllia-group parameters and compatibility. Confirmed: Hammer/Frogspawn/Anchor/Octospawn coexist; Torch Coral requires extra space due to stronger sweeper tentacles. General parameters for Euphyllia: pH 8.0–8.3; alkalinity 8–12 dKH; calcium 400–450 ppm; flow medium indirect; moderate lighting. Torch-specific parameters not broken out separately from Hammer/Frogspawn in this article. Used for Torch-vs-Hammer incompatibility confirmation.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World – Torch Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 78°F (25.5°C) steady; SG 1.025; pH 8.2; calcium 400 ppm; care level moderate; minimum tank 50 gal (189 L); adult size 4–5 inches (note: appears to describe a single head, not a mature multi-head colony); feeding weekly microplankton; lifespan up to 75 years; placement towards lower tank area. Salinity range, alkalinity, magnesium, and sweeper tentacle length not specified. Care level 'moderate' vs ReefChasers 'advanced' — 'advanced' adopted per task specification and disease/aggression management complexity.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Euphyllia glabrescens
additional.notes: Used for: taxonomy (family Euphylliidae, order Scleractinia; named Chamisso & Eysenhardt 1821), native range (Indo-Pacific, Red Sea to American Samoa), depth range (1–35 m), polyp morphology (corallites 20–30 mm, knob-tipped tubular tentacles), trade volume (Indonesia ~28,000/yr, Fiji ~6,000/yr in 2005), conservation status (Near Threatened IUCN 3.1). Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Euphyllia (genus / 2017 reclassification)
additional.notes: Used for: 2017 Luzon et al. molecular phylogenetics reclassification — documents which species stayed in Euphyllia (glabrescens, baliensis, cristata, paraglabrescens) vs. which moved to Fimbriaphyllia (ancora, divisa, paraancora, paradivisa, yaeyamaensis). Critical for documenting the Torch-stayed-in-Euphyllia fact and distinguishing it from the reclassified Hammer/Frogspawn species.
